title: Hormuz Tightens Again as IRGC Resumes Control, OPEC+ Unwinds Cuts, and Ukraine Reaches St. Petersburg
subhead: Iran's IRGC pushed back against ships in the Strait of Hormuz over the weekend, OPEC+ approved its fourth consecutive production hike, and Ukraine struck an oil terminal near St. Petersburg as Russia's domestic fuel crisis deepens.

sections.heading: Hormuz: IRGC Resumes Interdictions as Demining Timeline Stretches
sections.body:
Over the past 48 hours, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps appears to have tightened its grip on the Strait of Hormuz once again. According to The Guardian, at least eight ships turned around after direct IRGC warnings on Saturday, and the flow of transits on Sunday dropped further. Marine Traffic data showed 38 confirmed crossings on July 2, a 10% day-on-day decline. Iranian-flagged activity rose sharply to 11 crossings from two the previous day, while nine 'sanctioned' crossings were also observed, with route selection shifting toward Iranian and 'dark or unknown' corridors.

The IRGC's renewed assertiveness comes alongside the funeral of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and contradicts the US-Iran memorandum's requirement that Tehran use 'best endeavours' to restore pre-war traffic levels. A UKMTO advisory also reported a vessel in the Red Sea sending a distress signal after being attacked by 'unknown armed assailants' off Hodeidah.

France and Britain are advancing plans for a multinational mine-clearing naval mission for the southern Hormuz route, which Iran's foreign ministry rejected as 'unwarranted and unjustified interference.' A Saudi Gazette report estimated that demining operations could take 40–50 days before insurers and shipping companies feel confident enough to resume normal traffic. Oman is pushing a plan — loosely modeled on the Strait of Malacca — to charge optional navigational fees for safety, docking, and environmental services, with broad IMO support. Trump declared Hormuz would reopen 'toll-free,' but the 60-day ceasefire window is now ticking with both sides using the pause to reinforce military positions.
sections.whyItMatters: The IRGC's resumption of ship warnings shows that the US-Iran memorandum is a loose guideline, not an enforceable deal. Full Hormuz normalization is weeks away at best, and the dispute over tolls and governance could become a permanent feature of the waterway.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Approves Fourth Monthly Hike as Gulf Exports Recover
sections.body:
OPEC+ agreed on Sunday to increase output quotas by 188,000 barrels per day from August, the fourth consecutive monthly increase, according to CNBC. The seven core members — Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iraq, Kuwait, Algeria, Kazakhstan, and Oman — have now unwound roughly 800,000 bpd of the 1.65 million bpd cut agreed in 2023. With the UAE's departure from OPEC+ in late April, around 379,000 bpd of the original cut remains, meaning one more similar hike at the August 2 meeting could fully unwind the 2023 agreement.

OPEC+ output fell to 33.13 million bpd in May from 42.77 million bpd in February, per OPEC data, and has begun recovering in June. Brent crude traded near $72 per barrel on Friday — down from peaks above $120 — with Reuters polling analysts cutting 2026 oil price forecasts for the first time since the Iran war began. The Economist reports that despite the peace deal, prices are likely to stay elevated for months as physical supply chains and shipping routes normalize slowly.

Iraq is pressing for higher quotas, and the UAE's exit has complicated the group's internal politics. Meanwhile, Reuters reports that Asian crude spot premiums have already fallen back to pre-war levels, though shipping caution persists.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ is rapidly unwinding its production cuts just as Hormuz exports start flowing again — setting the stage for a potential oversupply in H2 2026. The question is whether demand, especially from China, will keep pace.

sections.heading: Ukraine Hits St. Petersburg Oil Terminal as Russian Fuel Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
Ukraine launched one of its longest-range drone attacks of the war overnight on July 5, striking an oil terminal in St. Petersburg's Kirovsky district and targeting military facilities at Kronstadt, according to AP. Russian officials said air defenses intercepted 72 drones over the Leningrad region. The strike came two days after Russia carried out one of its largest aerial bombardments of Kyiv.

The St. Petersburg Oil Terminal is one of Russia's principal Baltic petroleum export hubs. According to Reuters, Russia's western oil exports hit a record high in June as drone attacks on domestic refineries forced Moscow to boost crude exports instead of refined products. Russia has also lowered domestic fuel quality standards and introduced fuel purchase restrictions across multiple regions as shortages spread. Putin acknowledged the crisis on June 28, saying Russia held approximately 1.7 million metric tons of gasoline reserves while a task force works to stabilize production and distribution.

The Financial Times reports that Ukraine is now striking Russian refineries at a record pace, with US intelligence reportedly helping Kyiv bypass air defenses. Russia has begun advertising for drone operators to defend Moscow, according to Reuters.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone campaign is reshaping Russia's domestic fuel market and forcing a shift from refined product exports to crude exports — a structural change that undermines one of Russia's key revenue streams and creates cascading shortages at home.

sections.heading: Shadow Fleet Crackdown: UK Seizes Tanker, Europe Targets Cameroon Flag
sections.body:
British Royal Marines seized the Russian shadow fleet tanker Smyrtos in the English Channel on June 15 in a six-hour operation — the first time UK forces have boarded and captured such a vessel, according to BBC and The Telegraph. The operation, involving Chinook, Merlin, and Wildcat helicopters, an RAF P-8 aircraft, and two Royal Navy vessels, was ordered by Prime Minister Starmer. The Smyrtos had left Russia's Ust-Luga port on June 5. Its Indian captain, Ajay Pant, has been charged with contravening Russia sanctions. Starmer is now considering a plan to sell the seized oil to fund Ukraine's war effort.

Meanwhile, Europe is taking tougher action against shadow fleet tankers. Reuters reports that European authorities are boarding vessels at sea that falsely use Cameroon's flag registry. In response, Cameroon has dumped 39 such vessels from its ship registry. France separately fined the Tagor tanker €1 million after impounding it in May for shipping Russian oil. The EU adopted a new sanctions package adding 34 individuals and 47 entities, with a focus on energy revenue flows and state propaganda infrastructure. Canada announced sanctions targeting 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet and energy sector following a Carney-Zelensky meeting at the G7.

In the Baltic, Estonia released images showing machine guns and sandbags mounted on a Russian-flagged LNG carrier, signaling a more confrontational Moscow posture in protecting its civilian fleet near European waters.
sections.whyItMatters: The shadow fleet crackdown is escalating from sanctions lists to physical seizures — a significant escalation. Russia's arming of LNG carriers in the Baltic and the UK's first-ever tanker seizure raise the risk of direct confrontation at sea.

sections.heading: UAE's Dark Fleet: The Supertanker Tycoon Profiting From Hormuz Chaos
sections.body:
A Bloomberg/Sydney Morning Herald investigation reveals that the UAE quietly ran a covert operation to shuttle its crude out of the Strait of Hormuz using tactics normally associated with sanctioned states: ships travelling 'dark' without transponders, often under cover of darkness, offloading cargo via ship-to-ship transfers outside the waterway before returning for more. By the time the US-Iran interim deal was signed, the UAE was already approaching its pre-war export rate.

The operation relied heavily on South Korean shipping magnate Ga-Hyun Chung's Sinokor Group, which embarked on an unprecedented buying spree early this year. By June, nearly half of Emirati crude shipments were sailing on Sinokor-controlled vessels, according to Vortexa data. Broker estimates suggest just three tankers doing shuttle runs since mid-April could have earned Sinokor $60–120 million, with war premiums yielding three to four times pre-war rates.

Since the ceasefire, Sinokor has sent a further stream of supertankers into the Persian Gulf, and is now touting services to shipbrokers for cargoes beyond the UAE — expanding from a wartime workaround into a broader Gulf logistics business.
sections.whyItMatters: A US-aligned Gulf state adopted sanctions-evasion tactics to protect its oil exports — and built a profitable commercial model doing so. The Sinokor operation shows how wartime logistics innovations can persist into peacetime, reshaping tanker market dynamics.

sections.heading: India's Samudra Manthan: New Exploration Push After Energy Shock
sections.body:
India, the world's third-largest oil importer, is expanding domestic crude exploration after the Hormuz disruption exposed its import dependency. Oil Minister Hardeep Singh Puri told AFP that India is preparing to bid out 250,000 sq km of unexplored area under the 'Samudra Manthan' mission, including deepwater blocks off the Andaman and Nicobar archipelago. India survived the energy crunch by expanding its crude suppliers from 27 to 41 countries, including Iran, Venezuela, and more Russian and African purchases.

Domestic crude production meets just 10% of India's needs — roughly 522,000 bpd, well below the 2011 peak of 900,000 bpd. Puri says India is working with Petrobras, TotalEnergies, BP, Shell, and ExxonMobil on deepwater exploration. Separately, a $9 billion Great Nicobar Island megaport project is planned in the same Andaman Sea zone, creating a strategic base near the Malacca Strait approaches.

India's energy consumption is growing at three times the global pace, Puri said, rising from 5 million bpd in 2021 to 5.6 million bpd currently. The country also signed a tripartite agreement with Assam and Nagaland to resume oil exploration in disputed border areas after a three-decade hiatus.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz crisis has catalyzed India's most ambitious domestic exploration push in decades — but with demand growing at triple the global rate, even aggressive drilling won't meaningfully reduce import dependence. The strategic significance lies in the Andaman Sea, where India is simultaneously building a naval logistics hub.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Plan Stalls as Japan Pushes Alternative Stockpiling Model
sections.body:
The Trump administration's push to form a Western critical minerals trading bloc with regulated pricing is facing skepticism from G7 allies and a divided mining industry, Reuters reports. First proposed by VP JD Vance in February, the plan would use price supports, market standards, subsidies, and 'adjustable tariffs to uphold pricing integrity' to counter China's dominance in minerals like cobalt, lithium, and nickel. But G7 members have pushed back against USTR Jamieson Greer in private negotiations, particularly over a pricing mechanism derived from a Pentagon AI model. Key concerns center on who pays the premium, how far down the supply chain subsidies should go, and governance.

Meanwhile, Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi proposed a competing approach at the G7: a 'Joint Stockpiling Collaboration Initiative' for critical minerals, modeled on strategic petroleum reserves. Reuters reports that Canada and Japan are already working on joint mining projects with potential stockpiling cooperation. China's recent halt of rare earth exports to Japan has underscored the urgency — but also demonstrated Beijing's willingness to weaponize mineral supply chains.

The US mining industry is itself divided, with over 230 public submissions to Greer's office revealing disagreement on what steps the bloc should take. European officials are wary of a US-dominated pricing mechanism and prefer multilateral approaches.
sections.whyItMatters: Two competing models for Western critical minerals security are emerging: US price regulation via an AI-driven trading bloc vs. Japan's multilateral stockpiling approach. The division within the G7 mirrors the broader Western struggle to coordinate economic statecraft against China's mineral dominance.

bottomLine: The Strait of Hormuz is not returning to normal — the IRGC's weekend ship warnings show that Iran views the ceasefire memorandum as a flexible guideline, not a binding constraint, and the 40–50 day demining timeline means full traffic recovery is weeks away. Meanwhile, the energy market is being pulled in opposite directions: OPEC+ is unwinding cuts and adding supply just as demand uncertainty grows, Ukraine's St. Petersburg strike demonstrates that Russia's fuel crisis is structural and deepening, and the UAE's dark-fleet operation reveals how wartime logistics workarounds are becoming permanent commercial models. India's exploration push and the G7's stalled minerals bloc both reflect the same underlying trend: the Iran war has accelerated efforts to reduce import dependency, but the solutions on offer are slow and contested.
sources: The Guardian, CNBC, CryptoBriefing, Reuters, Bloomberg/Sydney Morning Herald, AP, BBC, The Telegraph, FT (via MSN), IBT, AFP/Nagaland Post/Economic Times, Estonia Defence Forces, Saudi Gazette, Asharq Al-Awsat, Global Banking & Finance/Reuters, Marine Traffic, UKMTO
